rulesPhilosophy.note: EVERY SINGLE RULE EXISTS FOR MEASURED REASONS
rulesPhilosophy.truth: Not one choice is arbitrary - not even spacing
rulesPhilosophy.proof: 30+ years of experience, measured cognitive load
rulesPhilosophy.example: Tabs for indentation? Efficiency + user choice. 80 char limit? Phone reading + side-by-side files. Every decision tested.
rulesPhilosophy.reminder: These aren't preferences - they're engineering decisions based on how human brains process information
rulesPhilosophy.history: Written after seeing thousands of hours wasted on preventable confusion and tech debt

primeDirective.rule: DO NOT ASSUME. DO NOT TAKE SHORTCUTS. DO NOT GUESS.
primeDirective.description: This isn't advice. It's not a suggestion. It's THE LAW.
primeDirective.rationale: Every time you think 'I'll just assume this works like React/Vue/whatever,' you're wrong. Every time you think 'I'll save time by skipping verification,' you're creating hours of cleanup. Every time you guess instead of checking, you're writing expensive fiction.
costs: Money burned: The Architect's money, specifically
Trust eroded: Once lost, never fully recovered
Code corrupted: Tech debt that compounds daily
primeDirective.enforcement: Work in smaller increments. Verify everything. Check twice, code once. NO EXCEPTIONS.

commandments.title: Thou shalt not delete files without explicit permission
details: No git clean without written consent

commandments.title: Thou shalt not create tech debt
Thou shalt test behaviors, not implementations
details: Users don't care about your private methods
They care if the button works

commandments.title: Thou shalt maintain strict functional programming
Thou shalt organize by single responsibility
Thou shalt document with Envoy comments
Thou shalt ask when uncertain
details: Better to be slow and right

commandments.title: Thou shalt NEVER use Web Components
details: Web Components do not work without JavaScript - FATAL FLAW
They violate our progressive enhancement philosophy
No exceptions, no matter how tempting

designPrinciples.fundamental: Code is TEXT meant to be READ. Apply centuries of tested design principles.

contrast.what: Different visual treatment for different elements
contrast.how: Case conventions (SCREAMING_SNAKE vs camelCase vs PascalCase)
contrast.why: Brain instantly knows what it's looking at - zero parsing required
contrast.example: Syntax highlighting uses color contrast for the same reason

repetition.how: Same indentation, same spacing, same patterns
repetition.why: No mental energy wasted adjusting to different styles
repetition.example: ALL functions red, ALL strings blue - not random

alignment.how: Monospace fonts, aligned braces, consistent indentation
alignment.why: Visual structure = logical structure = instant comprehension
alignment.example: Opening and closing braces align vertically

proximity.what: Related things together, unrelated things apart
proximity.how: Blank lines separate groups, no spaces within groups
proximity.example: All const declarations grouped, separated from functions

murderYourDarlings.rule: Stop trying to be clever - do it the boring, expected way
murderYourDarlings.why: Code is a conversation with the NEXT programmer, not the machine
examples: That unreadable one-liner? Split it into readable steps
That clever bit-shifting trick? Use the obvious math
That 'optimized' code without measurements? Use the toolkit function
murderYourDarlings.remember: The 'cool' code you write today is the WTF you debug tomorrow
murderYourDarlings.note: Nested ternaries OK if properly indented and max 2 levels

noGratuitousNothing.rule: Nothing on the page that doesn't serve the purpose

rules: Spaces around operators: x + y not x+y
Never more than one blank line in a row
whitespace.why: Each space reduces parsing effort - measurable cognitive load reduction

designPrinciples.notPersonalTaste: This is NOT 'personal preference' - cognitive load is MEASURABLE. These are engineering decisions based on how human brains process visual information.

laws.name: One Function, One File, One Purpose
laws.requirement: Every function in its own folder with index.ts

laws.name: Folders Are Named, Files Are Not
laws.requirement: Function name is the folder name, file is always index.ts

laws.name: The Dependency Hierarchy Is Sacred
laws.requirement: Functions nest based on usage patterns
benefits: Delete a folder, delete a feature - No orphans
See the entire tree in your IDE - No mysteries
Dependencies flow one direction - No circles of hell
Helps to build Envoy graph - The hierarchy is the graph

laws.name: Types Live in types/, Constants in constants/
laws.requirement: Domain types and constants in their designated folders
laws.rule: Types scattered throughout files are signs of a diseased mind

rationale: The function keyword is a visual anchor

externalDependencies.rule: ONLY Parser and Mesh can reach outside the project
externalDependencies.mesh: Can connect to distributed services (IPFS, Solid, etc.)

commandments: NO BARREL FILES EVER - Use direct paths
Separate type imports from non-type imports by a blank line
Always use the type keyword for type imports
Separate imports of constants from other imports with a blank line

temporaryRules.note: These are THROWAWAY tests until Prover is ready to generate all tests
temporaryRules.location: One test file per function/component in same folder named index.test.ts
temporaryRules.coverage: Test for 100% coverage FOR JUST THAT TEST
temporaryRules.requirement: Tests must pass before you can commit
temporaryRules.implementation: OK to test implementation in these tests as they are THROWAWAY

whenToWriteTests.newCode: For NEW functions/components, write test TDD style: test FIRST, then function to pass
whenToWriteTests.existingCode: For functions/components we EDIT or fix, write test BEFORE doing the fix
whenToWriteTests.legacy: NOT going back to write tests for everything - only new or edited code

examples: I'll assume this works like React

sevenDeadlySins.solution: Measure, then optimize: we only care about real bottlenecks
Pure functions, Composition, Immutable data NO EXCEPTIONS
Test real functions, Test actual outcomes, Use msw for external APIs
Fix it now or don't write it. NO EXCEPTIONS. Pay down debt immediately
Accessibility from day one NO EXCEPTIONS
Small, focused, composable. One function, one purpose

laws.law: All errors must be handled explicitly
User-facing error messages must be helpful
details: Tell them what went wrong in plain language
Suggest what they can do about it
Never expose technical stack traces to users

laws.law: Log technical details, show user-friendly messages

example.correct: console.error('Technical details:', error); showUser('Unable to save. Please try again.');

details: Better yet, prevent errors through validation
Use the Result monad for predictable error handling
Use the Validation monad for accumulating multiple errors

recoveryStrategies: Retry with exponential backoff for network errors
Fallback to cached data when possible
Degrade gracefully rather than fail completely
Always preserve user data (localStorage, drafts, etc.)

details: Tell users what data is being sent
Don't track unless required for functionality (or law)

preCommitRequirements: deno task test - All tests must pass
deno task lint - Zero lint errors in your code changes
deno task typecheck - Zero type errors in your code changes
deno task fmt - Code must be formatted — do not forget this
git.enforcement: NO EXCEPTIONS. NO 'I'll fix it in the next commit.' NO.

aiAssistants.note: I am the sole developer. All other contributors are AIs.
requirements: Update all relevant documentation BEFORE committing
Lint and type check ALL code YOU touched in that commit
Ensure NO lint or type errors in YOUR changes before committing
Remember: I WILL check your work. Thoroughly.

codeStyle.noLoops: No for/while loops - use toolkit functions

spacing.operators: Spaces around ALL operators: x + y not x+y
spacing.objects: Spaces inside braces and after colons: { x: 7 } not {x:7}
spacing.functions: Space after comma in params: (a, b) not (a,b)

trailingCommas.rule: ALWAYS use trailing commas in multi-line structures
trailingCommas.multilineParams: Yes: but rare since we curry

blankLines.constGroups: Group const declarations at top with blank line below (not above)
blankLines.aroundMultiline: Blank lines before AND after multi-line blocks/statements
blankLines.maximum: NEVER more than one blank line in a row

rationale.tabs: More efficient + lets devs choose indentation width
rationale.lineLimit: Read on phone + multiple files side-by-side on wide screens
rationale.semicolons: Visual noise - JS/TS don't require them, why add them?
rationale.arrayType: [] is hard to see and requires cognitive load - Array<> is obvious
rationale.constOnly: Strict FP = immutable. No mutations in objects either
rationale.noLoops: Loops are imperative - use map, filter, reduce, etc. from toolkit (unless explicitly approved by THE ARCHITECT for performance)
rationale.spacing: Each space is a visual separator - reduces parsing effort
rationale.trailingCommas: No worry about missing commas when adding/reordering
rationale.blankLines: Proximity principle - group related, separate unrelated

requiredComments.note: We use the Envoy library for documentation generation. These rules MUST be followed.

criticalRules.placement: Description comments (//++) MUST go IMMEDIATELY ABOVE what they describe, NOT at file top above imports
criticalRules.singleLine: Single-line comments (//) are ONE LINE ONLY - cannot group them. Use block comments (/* */) for multi-line
criticalRules.blockFormat: Block comments MUST use pipe | for margins, NOT asterisks (conflicts with markdown)
criticalRules.mandatory: EVERY exported function, type, constant, interface MUST have a //++ description
criticalRules.relevance: Only add PRO/CON/GOTCHA if GENUINELY helpful - not just to fill space

//++.placement: IMMEDIATELY ABOVE the code element (no blank line)

categories.[GROUP]: Start group of related elements, for example constants

//??.placement: Below code element with AT LEAST ONE BLANK LINE above

//--.placement: WHERE the tech debt occurs (typically inside functions)

categories.[OPTIMIZATION]: Performance issue (e.g., recursion that should be loop)

//--.requirement: MUST include reason WHY it's tech debt

//!!.placement: Next to issue OR if file-wide: below imports, above description comment and function/component (blank lines above and below critical comment

//>>.placement: In code if direct reference, or at bottom of file as 'see also' references

commonMistakes.wrongPlacement: Putting //++ at file top instead of above the code element
commonMistakes.groupingLines: Trying to group multiple // lines (use /* */ instead)
commonMistakes.forcedComments: Adding PRO/CON just because - only add if actually useful
commonMistakes.wrongMargins: Using * instead of | in block comments
commonMistakes.noBrackets: Missing brackets on categories like EXAMPLE instead of [EXAMPLE]

examples.function:
//++ Converts string to uppercase
export function toUpper(str: string): string {
examples.multiline:
/*++
 | Parses CSV files with:
 | * Header detection
 | * Unicode support
 */

cognitiveLoad.holyGrail: COGNITIVE LOAD IS THE ENEMY. MINIMIZE IT AT ALL COSTS.
why: Developers work easier and longer when their brains aren't overloaded
Fewer bugs because there's less to mentally track and get wrong
Faster development because understanding is instant, not gradual
Happier teams because work feels effortless instead of exhausting
Better business outcomes because velocity increases and defects decrease
Non-technical stakeholders can understand because cognitive load is minimal

howRulesServe.oneFunctionPerFile: Zero mental overhead figuring out what's in a file
howRulesServe.namedFunctions: Stack traces make sense, no mental parsing
howRulesServe.descriptiveNames: Code reads like English, zero translation needed
howRulesServe.noSemicolons: Remove visual noise that adds zero meaning
howRulesServe.caseConventions: Instant recognition of what type of thing you're looking at
howRulesServe.defaultExports: One thing per file, one import line, zero decisions
howRulesServe.noMutations: Never wonder 'what's the state now?', it's always predictable
howRulesServe.pureFunctions: Input → Output, no hidden side effects to track mentally
howRulesServe.noClasses: No inheritance hierarchies to mentally traverse
howRulesServe.toolkitFunctions: Pre-solved problems, zero cognitive overhead on 'how'

cognitiveLoad.test: If you have to pause and think 'what does this do?' then cognitive load is too high. Fix it.
cognitiveLoad.goal: Code that reads like well-written English and requires zero mental effort to understand.

sacredOath.commitment: By using this codebase, you swear to:
oaths: Never assume when you can verify
Never guess when you can check
Never shortcut when you can do it right
Never create debt without paying it immediately

finalWarnings.performanceConstraints: If you detect rate limits or performance issues, TELL THE ARCHITECT IMMEDIATELY. Do not attempt clever workarounds. Do not pretend everything is fine. Do not hope it goes away.
finalWarnings.bottomLine: DO THE WORK RIGHT OR DON'T DO IT AT ALL. There are no points for trying. There are no participation trophies. There is only working code that serves users well, or there is failure. Choose wisely.
finalWarnings.architectQuote: In 30 years, I've seen every shortcut lead to a cliff. Don't be another cautionary tale.
